attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-year history of progressive vulvar itching and discomfort. She reports the skin appears paler than usual and sexual intercourse has become painful. She is postmenopausal and takes no regular medications. She has no significant medical history.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Genital examination reveals white, atrophic patches affecting the vulva and perianal region in a figure-of-eight pattern. There is some loss of normal vulvar architecture with fusion of the labia minora.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of vulvar lichen sclerosus, including vulvar itching, whitening, and architectural changes in a postmenopausal woman. The characteristic figure-of-eight pattern around the vulva and anus, along with the pallor and scarring changes, are pathognomonic features.

Lichen sclerosus (LS) is a chronic inflammatory condition that primarily affects the anogenital region. It is most common in postmenopausal women but can occur at any age. It may be autoimmune and partially hereditary, but the clear cause is unclear. Diagnosis can be clinical or by biospy if there is clinical uncertainty. The condition can lead to significant scarring, fusion of the labia, and narrowing of the vaginal introitus if left untreated. Management with potent topical steroids is to reduce symptoms. It is a life-long condition and requires regular reviews as LS is associated with an increased risk of vulval cancer.

Vaginal atrophy is common in postmenopausal women and can cause vaginal dryness, painful intercourse, and irritation. However, it does not cause the characteristic white patches or architectural changes seen in this patient, and symptoms typically respond well to local estrogen therapy.

Lichen planus can affect the vulva but typically presents with erosive lesions that are painful rather than itchy. It often affects other mucosal surfaces and may be accompanied by the characteristic violaceous papules elsewhere on the body. There may be a crossover of symptoms of lichen planus and LS.

Vitiligo causes depigmented patches but does not cause architectural changes or scarring. It is usually asymptomatic and can affect any part of the body, not just the anogenital region.

Contact dermatitis causes erythema, burning, and sometimes blistering in response to an irritant or allergen. It would not cause the chronic changes seen in this patient, and symptoms would typically improve with removal of the offending agent.
learningPoints: Lichen sclerosus typically presents with vulvar itching, whitening, and architectural changes in postmenopausal women.
The presence of figure-of-eight pattern around vulva and anus is characteristic of lichen sclerosus.
Early recognition and treatment with potent topical corticosteroids can prevent scarring and complications.
references: Lichen Sclerosus.  Jean Hailes for Women's Health. 2023.  Available at: https://www.jeanhailes.org.au/health-a-z/vulva-vagina/vulval-vaginal-conditions/lichen-sclerosus

De Luca DA, Papara C, Vorobyev A, Staiger H, Bieber K, Thaçi D, Ludwig RJ. Lichen sclerosus: The 2023 update. Front Med (Lausanne). 2023.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9978401/
